text:
Carlos Alcarazmay have been beaten by old foeNovak Djokovicin the men's singles final of theOlympics, but he'll receive a decent reward for hissilver medal. Alcaraz ran out victorious when the two met at the finals ofWimbledonthis summer, beating the tennis great 6-2, 6-2, 7-6 onCentre Courtafter an impressive tournament.

But Djokovic had his revenge at this summer'sOlympics in Paris, beating the Spaniard 7-6, 7-6 to add a gold medal to his 24 career Grand Slam wins. And while Alcaraz will no doubt be unhappy to take home silver instead of gold, he'll receive a decent amount from his country for a respectable second-place finish.

That's because theSpanish Olympic Comittee (COE)award €48,000 (£41,206) to silver medallists in individual competitions. The 21-year-old would have been €94,000 (£80,683) better off had he been successful in defeating Djokovic, while a third-place finish still would have generously handed him €30,000 (£25,751) from the singles competition.

Of course, the Olympic prize money that Alcaraz will receive for his efforts is a far cry from the money he bagged from hisWimbledonwin earlier this summer. The south-west London Grand Slam paid out £2.7million to its victor, while Djokovic was awarded £1.4million for his second-place finish.

It is reported that Alcaraz has already racked up a staggering net worth of around $15million (£11.7million) through his tennis endeavours and lucrative sponsorship deals. The youngster has 15ATP Tour titlesand fourGrand Slamsto his name in the form of a2022 US Openwin,2023 and 2024 Wimbledonvictories, and aFrench Opensuccess earlier this year.

In terms of sponsorships, meanwhile, Alcaraz has worked with the likes of Nike, Babolat, Rolex, ElPozo, BMW and Calvin Klein to name a few. The Spanish sensation's road to the Olympic final was fairly straightforward, save a potentially tricky semi-final affair.

Our community members are treated to special offers, promotions, and adverts from us and our partners. You can check out at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Alcaraz took on Hady Habib in his opening game, before battling with Tallon Griekspoor in the second round. A victory over Roman Safiullin saw him advance to a quarter final with Team USA's Tommy Paul, winning 6-3, 7-6 to make it to the semis against Felix Auger Aliassime.

He triumphed 6-1, 6-1 against the Canadian, before giving it his all against Djokovic in the final. Unfortunately it wasn't his day on the clay at Roland-Garros, with Djokovic reigning supreme and denying the youngster of his third landmark achievement of the year after the French Open and Wimbledon.

Alcaraz had to cut short his on-court interview with Alex Corretja after the loss, struggling to get his words out through the tears of coming so close to Olympic greatness. He was consoled by the journalist, who put his arm around Alcaraz before giving him space and continuing his analysis of the match solo.



Alcaraz would later return to say: "I gave everything," before conceding he was bettered by his opponent. He added: "It’s painful to lose the way I lost this match," going on: "I had my chances to probably be up in the match. I couldn’t take it. Novak was playing great. He deserves this. In the tough moments he increased his level.

"He played unbelievable shots... an unbelievable game. I’m a bit disappointed but honestly I’ll leave the court with my head really really high. Fighting for Spain was everything to me. I’m proud with the way I played today."

title: Carlos Alcaraz's Olympic earnings for silver medal as star breaks down | Tennis | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Aaron Morris
description: Carlos Alcaraz may have been beaten in the men's singles final in Paris, but the Spaniard will still take home a pretty penny for his silver medal
